title: CodegenFilePlan
description: The inbound message across the code-generation seam (spec §5.2): a language backend's answer to a CodegenManifest. A backend is any process that reads the manifest (the whole oracle by value) on stdin and writes this file plan on stdout — a flat list of files to write, each by value. The consumer materialises the plan. PROVENANCE IS THE CONSUMER'S JOB, NOT THE BACKEND'S: the consumer (not the backend) appends the provenance manifest (§7.3.1) to what it writes, so the drift gate covers EVERY backend identically — a backend cannot opt out of provenance or forge it, because it never writes provenance at all. Paths are RELATIVE and CONTAINED: no absolute path (no leading `/`) and no `..` segment may escape the output root; the `path` pattern enforces this structurally and the consumer rejects any plan that violates it. Self-contained: no cross-file `$ref`.

files.description: The files the backend asks the consumer to write, each by value. Order is not semantically significant; two entries targeting the same `path` are a backend defect the consumer may reject.

path.description: The output-root-RELATIVE path to write. CONTAINED: the pattern rejects a leading `/` (no absolute paths) and any `..` path segment (no escaping the output root), so a plan can never write outside the tree the consumer owns. Forward slashes separate segments.

content.description: The file body, by value. A generated file typically carries a graph-hash provenance header the backend renders from the manifest's `graph_hash`; the consumer additionally appends the provenance manifest (§7.3.1) — the backend does not write that.

overwrite.description: Whether the consumer may overwrite an existing file at `path` on a later run. Optional; effectively defaults to TRUE — generated files are regenerated every run and the drift gate keeps them honest. Set `overwrite: false` to mark a REALISER-OWNED SCAFFOLD: written once if absent, then never regenerated (e.g. an entry point or config a human is expected to edit), so the backend seeds it without ever clobbering local edits.
